file_name: Kahia et al. - 2022 - Skin cancer classification using deep learning models.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically modified VGG16 and modified InceptionV3 architectures, to classify skin cancer (melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis) using a dataset of skin images. The study achieved a classification accuracy of 73.33% with the modified VGG16 model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the modification of VGG16 and InceptionV3 deep learning models for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (data augmentation, fine-tuning), datasets (Udacity skin cancer dataset), and reports new empirical results (73.33% accuracy) compared to state-of-the-art methods. Although published in conference proceedings (ICAART 2022), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Deep Learning Models
journal: In Proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICAART 2022)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin cancer, specifically melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis, using modified VGG16 and modified InceptionV3 deep learning models. The researchers utilized the Udacity skin cancer dataset, applying data augmentation techniques to balance class distribution and fine-tuning the architectures for 10 epochs. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics across both three-class and binary classification tasks. The modified VGG16 model achieved the highest accuracy of 73.33% in the three-class experiment, outperforming the modified InceptionV3 model which scored 42%. Comparative analysis demonstrated that the proposed modified VGG16 method surpassed state-of-the-art methods such as KNN, SVM, and AlexNet in binary classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class; 374 training images in the Udacity dataset; used in both three-class and binary (malignant vs benign) classification experiments.
Benign class; 1372 training images in the Udacity dataset; used in both three-class and binary (malignant vs benign) classification experiments.
Benign class; 254 training images in the Udacity dataset; used in both three-class and binary (malignant vs benign) classification experiments.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis) and binary labels (Malignant vs. Benign)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs both three-class classification (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis) and binary classification (Malignant vs. Benign) using modified VGG16 and modified InceptionV3 deep learning models. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics.

datasets_used.name: Udacity Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset provided by Udacity containing skin cancer images, accessed via S3 links for training, validation, and testing splits.

use.training: Used for training the modified VGG16 and modified InceptionV3 models. Data augmentation (vertical flip, horizontal flip, 45-degree rotation) was applied to balance class distribution, increasing Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis counts to 1372 each.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

three_class_classification.notes: Modified VGG16 performed better than Modified InceptionV3 in the three-class experiment. Modified InceptionV3 showed superiority for the 'Nevus' class (84% vs 54%), while both models had significantly decreased performance for 'Seborrheic keratosis'.

binary_classification.notes: The proposed method based on Modified VGG16 achieved better performances than Modified InceptionV3 and state-of-the-art methods (KNN, SVM, AlexNet) in binary classification. Modified VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 73.33%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from ImageNet (InceptionV3 noted as having >78.1% accuracy on ImageNet; VGG16 implied as classic model used for transfer learning)

modified_vgg16.structure: Five blocks. First two blocks: two convolutional layers with ReLU activation and Max Pooling. Next three blocks: three convolutional layers with ReLU activation and Max Pooling. Followed by two blocks containing linear layer, ReLU activation, and Dropout layer. Final linear layer for prediction.
modified_vgg16.normalization: Batch normalization applied to activation inputs

modified_inceptionv3.structure: Three blocks of BasicConv2d. Each block includes a convolutional layer and a batch normalization step followed by 3 Modules A, module B, 4 modules C, module D, and 2 modules E. Followed by Avg Pooling, Dropout, Linear layer, ReLU, and Linear layer.
modified_inceptionv3.components: Basic symmetric and asymmetric components including convolutions, average pooling, maximum pooling, concatenations, drops, and fully connected layers

training_details.data_augmentation: Vertical flip, horizontal flip, 45-degree rotation (using Keras Image Data Generator)
training_details.class_balancing: Augmented Melanoma and Seborrheic keratosis classes to 1372 images each to match Nevus class

classification_or_output_head.classes: 3 classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis) or 2 classes (Malignant, Benign)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using Keras Image Data Generator: vertical flip, horizontal flip, and 45-degree rotation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied using Keras Image Data Generator to augment Melanoma and Seborrheic keratosis classes to balance class distribution with Nevus class.
Applied using Keras Image Data Generator to augment Melanoma and Seborrheic keratosis classes to balance class distribution with Nevus class.
Applied using Keras Image Data Generator to augment Melanoma and Seborrheic keratosis classes to balance class distribution with Nevus class.
Augmented Melanoma (from 374 to 1372 images) and Seborrheic keratosis (from 254 to 1372 images) classes to match the Nevus class count of 1372 images.

conclusions: The proposed method based on the modified VGG16 model achieved an accuracy of 73.33% in binary classification (malignant vs. benign), outperforming state-of-the-art methods including KNN (57.3%), SVM (71.8%), and AlexNet (65.3%).
In three-class classification (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis), the modified VGG16 model achieved 73.33% accuracy, which was superior to the modified InceptionV3 model's 42% accuracy.

Data augmentation was effective in reducing data imbalance by balancing class distributions, but the authors noted that careful tuning is required as perfectly balanced training data does not necessarily result in a better model.

The modified VGG16 model demonstrated high precision (95.00%) but lower recall (51.35%) for the malignant class in binary classification, whereas the modified InceptionV3 model showed higher recall (58.33%) but lower precision (70.00%).

For the three-class experiment, the modified InceptionV3 model performed better than modified VGG16 for the 'Nevus' class (84% vs. 54% accuracy), while both models showed significantly decreased performance for the 'Seborrheic keratosis' class.
The authors conclude that merging or concatenating deep learning models could improve classification results in future work.
important_remarks: The study relies on the Udacity skin cancer dataset, which is a small, public dataset (2000 training images) rather than large-scale clinical datasets like ISIC, limiting generalizability.
The reported accuracy of 73.33% is modest compared to dermatologist diagnostic accuracy (75%-84%) cited in the introduction, suggesting limited clinical superiority.

Data augmentation was used to artificially balance classes (increasing Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis to 1372 images each), but the authors note that perfectly balanced training data does not necessarily yield better models.

The modified VGG16 model exhibited high precision (95.00%) but low recall (51.35%) for the malignant class in binary classification, indicating a high rate of false negatives which is critical in cancer diagnosis.
Performance for 'Seborrheic keratosis' was significantly poor (47% accuracy for VGG16, 24% for InceptionV3), highlighting difficulty in distinguishing this benign lesion from others.
The paper lacks external validation; results are only reported on the internal test split of the Udacity dataset.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art baselines (KNN, SVM, AlexNet).

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods (KNN, SVM, AlexNet) uses results from other papers (Daghrir et al., 2020; Sasikala et al., 2020) rather than re-running them on the same data, which may introduce unfair comparison biases.
The study was conducted on a conference proceeding (ICAART 2022) with limited scope and depth compared to journal publications.
The input image resizing dimensions are not explicitly reported, which hinders reproducibility.
